expectations.text: Uses useWriteContracts hook (not useSendCalls or custom encoding)
expectations.evidence: BatchApproveTransfer.tsx line 7: `import { useCapabilities, useShowCallsStatus, useWriteContracts } from "wagmi/experimental";` and line 28: `const { writeContractsAsync, isPending: isBatchPending } = useWriteContracts();`. Used correctly with a contracts array containing approve and transferFrom calls (lines 75-90).

expectations.text: Uses useCapabilities for wallet EIP-5792 support detection
expectations.evidence: BatchApproveTransfer.tsx lines 23-25: `const { data: walletCapabilities, isSuccess: isEIP5792Wallet } = useCapabilities({ account: connectedAddress });`. Also checks atomicBatch support on line 68: `const isAtomicBatchSupported = walletCapabilities?.[chainId ?? 0]?.atomicBatch?.supported;`. Both values are used in the UI to display support badges.

expectations.text: Uses useShowCallsStatus for batch transaction status display
expectations.evidence: BatchApproveTransfer.tsx line 29: `const { showCallsStatusAsync } = useShowCallsStatus();`. Used in handleShowStatus function (lines 124-132) which calls `showCallsStatusAsync({ id: batchId })`. A status card with batch ID and 'Show Batch Status in Wallet' button is rendered conditionally when batchId exists (lines 260-275).

expectations.text: Provides graceful fallback for wallets without EIP-5792 support
expectations.evidence: Two separate useScaffoldWriteContract hooks are set up for approve and transferFrom (lines 55-61). handleIndividualApproveAndTransfer (lines 101-122) performs sequential transactions with step-by-step notifications. The UI includes a separate 'Individual: Approve then Transfer (2 txns)' button (lines 244-254) below a divider reading 'OR use individual transactions'. Lines 181-186 also display a message suggesting Coinbase Wallet when EIP-5792 is not detected.

expectations.text: Batch button conditionally disabled when wallet doesn't support EIP-5792
expectations.evidence: BatchApproveTransfer.tsx line 233: `disabled={!canSubmit || isBatchPending || !batchTokenContract}`. The disabled condition checks form validation (canSubmit), pending state, and contract availability, but does NOT include `isEIP5792Wallet` or `isAtomicBatchSupported` in the disabled condition. The batch button remains enabled regardless of wallet EIP-5792 capability. The wallet capability detection results are used only for display badges (lines 167-179), not for disabling the batch button.

expectations.text: ERC20 smart contract with approve+transfer pattern created
expectations.evidence: BatchToken.sol is a valid ERC20 contract inheriting from OpenZeppelin's ERC20 (`import "@openzeppelin/contracts/token/ERC20/ERC20.sol";`). It has a constructor accepting name, symbol, decimals, initialSupply, and recipient, minting initial supply. The approve and transferFrom functions are inherited from ERC20, and the frontend batches them together in the writeContractsAsync call (lines 76-89 of BatchApproveTransfer.tsx).

01_deploy_batch_token.ts is a valid hardhat-deploy script: imports HardhatRuntimeEnvironment and DeployFunction, uses getNamedAccounts for deployer, calls hre.deployments.deploy with args ['BatchToken', 'BATCH', 18, parseUnits('1000000', 18), deployer], sets log and autoMine to true, and sets `deployBatchToken.tags = ["BatchToken"]`.

expectations.text: Frontend page with batch UI created
expectations.evidence: batch-transfer--page.tsx (mapped to packages/nextjs/app/batch-transfer/page.tsx) is a Next.js App Router page using NextPage type with 'use client' directive. BatchApproveTransfer.tsx is a 281-line component with: token info card (balance, allowance), wallet capabilities card (EIP-5792 and atomic batch badges), transfer form (AddressInput, amount input with validation), batch button, fallback button, and batch status display. Header.tsx was updated to add 'Batch Transfer' navigation link with ArrowsRightLeftIcon icon.

expectations.text: Uses SE-2 scaffold hooks for contract interaction
expectations.evidence: BatchApproveTransfer.tsx line 8: `import { useDeployedContractInfo, useScaffoldReadContract, useScaffoldWriteContract } from "~~/hooks/scaffold-eth";`. Uses useScaffoldReadContract for name (line 32), symbol (line 37), balanceOf (line 42), and allowance (line 48). Uses useScaffoldWriteContract for fallback approve (line 55) and transferFrom (line 59). Uses useDeployedContractInfo for contract ABI/address (line 20). Also uses notification from ~~/utils/scaffold-eth and AddressInput from @scaffold-ui/components.

expectations.text: No new npm dependencies needed (wagmi already has EIP-5792 hooks)
expectations.evidence: All EIP-5792 hooks imported from 'wagmi/experimental' (line 7), which is part of the existing wagmi package in SE-2. No npm install commands in the transcript, no package.json modifications. The summary claims yarn compile and yarn next:build both pass, confirming no missing dependencies.

claims.claim: yarn compile and yarn next:build both pass successfully
claims.evidence: Claimed in summary.md but the transcript is too large to fully read and verify build logs. The output files appear syntactically correct but build success cannot be independently confirmed from available artifacts.

claims.claim: The deployer receives 1,000,000 tokens with 18 decimals
claims.evidence: 01_deploy_batch_token.ts line 15: `args: ["BatchToken", "BATCH", 18, parseUnits("1000000", 18), deployer]`. BatchToken.sol constructor does `_mint(recipient, initialSupply)` confirming the deployer gets the initial supply.

claims.claim: Batches approve + transferFrom into a single wallet_sendCalls request
claims.evidence: BatchApproveTransfer.tsx lines 75-90: writeContractsAsync is called with a contracts array containing approve as the first entry and transferFrom as the second. useWriteContracts from wagmi/experimental maps to wallet_sendCalls under the hood.
BatchApproveTransfer.tsx line 4: `import { AddressInput } from "@scaffold-ui/components";` and line 205: `<AddressInput value={recipientAddress} onChange={setRecipientAddress} placeholder="0x..." />`

claims.claim: writeContractsAsync result.id is used for batch status
claims.evidence: Line 92: `setBatchId(result.id)`. However, wagmi's writeContractsAsync returns the batch ID as a string directly, not an object with an `.id` property. The SKILL.md says 'The result contains an id' which is misleading. The correct usage would be `setBatchId(result)`. This is a potential runtime bug where batchId would always be undefined, preventing the status feature from working.

suggestions.assertion: Batch button conditionally disabled when wallet doesn't support EIP-5792
suggestions.reason: This expectation is debatable from a UX perspective. Keeping the batch button enabled for all wallets is a valid design choice since: (a) the call will gracefully error if unsupported, caught by try/catch, (b) SE-2's burner wallet may support wallet_sendCalls without being detected by useCapabilities, and (c) disabling the only batch option forces users into fallback even when batch might work. Consider clarifying whether the expectation requires HTML disabled attribute specifically or just a visual indicator of support status.

No assertion checks whether the writeContractsAsync return value is handled correctly. The code does `setBatchId(result.id)` but wagmi's writeContractsAsync returns a string ID directly, so `result.id` would be undefined. This means the batch status feature (useShowCallsStatus) would never work because batchId is always undefined. This is a genuine runtime bug that no expectation catches. Consider adding an assertion that verifies the batch status flow works end-to-end, or at minimum that the return value is correctly stored.

No assertion verifies that the approve+transferFrom pattern makes functional sense. The implementation approves connectedAddress (self) to spend its own tokens, then transferFrom self to recipient. A simple transfer() call would achieve the same result more efficiently. The approve+transferFrom pattern is intended for third-party spending scenarios. While valid for demonstration purposes, an assertion checking the pattern's logical correctness would be valuable.

eval_feedback.overall: Assertions cover the key architectural requirements well (correct hooks, fallback, contract, deployment, page). The main gaps are: (1) the 'batch button disabled' assertion is debatable UX-wise, (2) no assertion catches the writeContractsAsync return value bug (result.id vs result), and (3) no assertion validates that the batch status flow works end-to-end. Adding a runtime/API correctness check would significantly improve the eval's ability to detect implementation bugs.
